nativeRange.habitat: Found around isolated coral patches on sandy and muddy slopes, typically at depths of 15–44 m; occasionally enters shallow estuaries. Adults are generally observed in pairs swimming close to the substrate near remote coral heads, and have been reported to engage in cleaner-fish behavior on larger reef fish.

diet.notes: Has been documented preferring live zooplankton — copepods, amphipods, brine shrimp nauplii, cyclops, and small mysis. Wild-caught specimens have been reported to often refuse prepared and frozen foods initially, frequently leading to starvation; aquacultured specimens are generally considered far easier to feed and tend to accept enriched frozen mysis and vitamin-soaked brine shrimp. Generally recommended to feed small portions 3–4 times daily and to maintain a healthy resident pod population in the display or a connected refugium.

summary: A strikingly banded reef pipefish from the Coral Triangle, generally considered expert-only due to its specialized live-food requirements and intolerance of competitive or boisterous tankmates.
careNotes: The Yellow Multibanded Pipefish (Dunckerocampus pessuliferus) is a slow, deliberate swimmer that tends to spend much of its time near overhangs, caves, and gorgonian-style decor, and has been reported to act as a facultative cleaner of larger fish in the wild. Best kept as a mated pair or small group in a dedicated, mature reef aquarium with stable parameters, gentle flow, and a strong resident copepod/amphipod population. Wild-collected specimens have a poor survival record in captivity and are commonly considered an expert-only species; aquacultured specimens have been reported to be substantially hardier and easier to feed and are strongly recommended where available. Generally not suited to mixed community reefs — fast-moving or aggressive tankmates tend to outcompete pipefish for food, and large-polyp corals, anemones, large shrimp, and crabs have been reported to harm or prey on them. Tank-size sources disagree widely (LiveAquaria 50 US gal/190 L, Reefapp 100 L, Reeflex 250 L); a 200 L footprint with at least ~90 cm of length is used here, biased above the retailer minimum given the species' 16 cm adult length, slow feeding, and need for swimming space and dedicated husbandry. LiveAquaria tags the species as 'venomous', but FishBase, Wikipedia, and reviewed Syngnathidae literature do not document venomous structures in Dunckerocampus pessuliferus; venomousSpines is set to false pending evidence to the contrary. Temperature sources span 22–28.9°C across LiveAquaria, Reeflex, Reefapp, and FishBase environmental data; an averaged 23–27°C range is used.
breedingNotes: Reproduction is ovoviviparous. Following a courtship dance, the female deposits adhesive eggs onto a flat brooding area on the underside of the male's trunk/tail, where the male oxygenates and broods them until release. Pairs are typically monogamous. Captive breeding has been reported and aquacultured juveniles are commercially available; rearing fry is generally considered advanced and requires a sustained supply of very small live zooplankton.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Species product page reached via the pipefish collection root. Provided care-level, water-parameter, tank-size, and reef-compatibility figures.

additional.name: theaquariumwiki.com
additional.notes: Assigned secondary returned an Anubis access-denied page (403-equivalent). Fell back through the slice's secondary list.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Confirmed taxonomy (Syngnathidae, Syngnathiformes), max size 16 cm TL, depth range 1–44 m, Indo-West Pacific range, ovoviviparous reproduction, and environmental temperature 24.1–28.9°C (mean 27.6).

additional.name: Wikipedia
additional.notes: Confirmed current genus placement under Dunckerocampus, Coral Triangle distribution (Philippines, Indonesia, NW Australia), depth 15–44 m, cleaner behavior, and ovoviviparous brood-pouch biology.

additional.name: ReefApp Encyclopedia
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size 16 cm, ~26°C target, SG ~1.026, lifespan 2–3 years, peaceful temperament, captive-bred availability, and need for overhangs/caves and gentle flow. Listed minimum tank as 100 L — used as low-end anchor in the tank-size disagreement.

additional.name: Reeflex
additional.notes: Cross-checked size 16 cm, temperature 22–28°C, 250 L minimum recommendation, advanced-only care, and live-food bias. Used as high-end anchor in the tank-size disagreement.

fish.breedingNotes: Captive pairs have been reported to spawn in mature reef aquariums with stable parameters and abundant live food; raising fry is generally considered advanced and requires a continuous supply of very small live zooplankton.
